0.title: AI Recommendation Generated

databaseQueries.query: SELECT `permissions`.*, `model_has_permissions`.`model_id` as `pivot_model_id`, `model_has_permissions`.`permission_id` as `pivot_permission_id`, `model_has_permissions`.`model_type` as `pivot_model_type` FROM `permissions` inner join `model_has_permissions` on `permissions`.`id` = `model_has_permissions`.`permission_id` WHERE `model_has_permissions`.`model_id` in (8) and `model_has_permissions`.`model_type` = 'App\\Models\\User'

SELECT `roles`.*, `model_has_roles`.`model_id` as `pivot_model_id`, `model_has_roles`.`role_id` as `pivot_role_id`, `model_has_roles`.`model_type` as `pivot_model_type` FROM `roles` inner join `model_has_roles` on `roles`.`id` = `model_has_roles`.`role_id` WHERE `model_has_roles`.`model_id` in (8) and `model_has_roles`.`model_type` = 'App\\Models\\User'
SELECT * FROM `agricultural_professionals` WHERE `agricultural_professionals`.`user_id` = 8 and `agricultural_professionals`.`user_id` IS not NULL LIMIT 1
SELECT * FROM `soil_analysis` WHERE exists (SELECT * FROM `farmers` WHERE `soil_analysis`.`farmer_id` = `farmers`.`id` and `farmer_address_mun` = '05') and `soil_analysis`.`id` = '45' LIMIT 1

UPDATE `soil_analysis` SET `ai_diagnosis` = '', `ai_farmer_summary` = '', `ai_key_concerns` = '[\"Concern 1: The most critical issue IS the very high soil pH of 8.1, which IS far too alkaline for Arabica coffee (optimal 5.5-6.5). This high pH makes vital nutrients like Phosphorus, Potassium, Iron, Manganese, and Zinc unavailable to the coffee plants, leading to stunted growth, yellowing leaves (chlorosis), and overall poor plant vigor despite the high organic matter and nitrogen levels.\",\"Concern 2: The extremely low Potassium (K) level of 10 ppm will severely impact coffee cherry development, bean filling, and overall yield quality. Plants will exhibit weak stems, poor disease resistance, and leaf margins may show yellowing or scorching, leading to small, light, and poor-quality coffee beans.\",\"Concern 3: The low Phosphorus (P) level of 5 ppm IS detrimental to coffee root development, flowering, and fruit SET. Coffee plants will have a weak root system, reduced flower production, and poor fruit retention, resulting in significantly lower yields and delayed maturity of coffee cherries.\"]', `ai_priority_actions` = '[\"Priority 1: Immediately apply agricultural grade Elemental Sulfur to lower the soil pH. Apply 50-100 grams per coffee plant, incorporated around the drip line, or 500-1000 kg per hectare. This product can be purchased FROM local agri-vet stores; estimated cost IS PHP 50-100 per kg, so around PHP 2,500-10,000 for 50-100 kg for initial treatment depending on farm size.\",\"Priority 2: Within the next two weeks, apply Muriate of Potash (0-0-60) to address the very low potassium. Apply 100-150 grams per coffee plant, split INTO two applications during the fruit development stage (e.g., May-June and August-September). This IS available at agri-vet stores for approximately PHP 50-70 per kg.\",\"Priority 3: Within the next 2\\u20134 weeks, apply Solophos (0-20-0) or Superphosphate (0-18-0) to correct the low phosphorus. Apply 100-150 grams per coffee plant, banded around the drip line, ideally before the main flowering flush (e.g., March-April). These are available at agri-vet stores for approximately PHP 40-60 per kg.\"]', `ai_soil_remarks` = '{\"ph\":\"The High pH level of 8.1 indicates an alkaline soil, which IS highly unfavorable for Coffee - Arabica as it drastically reduces the availability of crucial nutrients like Phosphorus, Potassium, Iron, and Manganese, impairing root health and overall plant vigor. To correct this, the farmer must apply agricultural grade Elemental Sulfur at a rate of 50-100 grams per plant, incorporated INTO the soil around the drip line, to gradually lower the pH towards the optimal range of 5.5-6.5.\",\"om\":\"The Very High organic matter content of 10% IS excellent, significantly improving soil structure, water retention capacity, and fostering robust microbial activity, all beneficial for Coffee - Arabica. To maintain this superb level, the farmer should continue applying mature compost or vermicast at a rate of 1-2 kilograms per plant annually, ensuring a continuous supply of slow-release nutrients and sustained soil health.\",\"n\":\"The Very High nitrogen level of 8.1% IS excessive and can lead to undesirable effects such as overly lush vegetative growth at the expense of flowering and fruiting, making coffee plants more susceptible to pests and diseases. No additional nitrogen fertilizer IS recommended at this time; instead, focus on balancing the other deficient nutrients to promote reproductive growth and avoid further N accumulation.\",\"p\":\"The Low phosphorus level of 5 ppm will severely hinder coffee root development, reduce the intensity of flowering, and negatively impact fruit SET, leading to lower yields and poor plant establishment. To correct this, apply Solophos (0-20-0) or Superphosphate (0-18-0) at a rate of 100-150 grams per plant, applied by banding around the drip line before the main flowering season (March-April) to ensure nutrient availability for reproductive growth.\",\"k\":\"The Very Low potassium level of 10 ppm will critically affect coffee cherry filling, bean density, overall quality, and the plant\'s resistance to diseases and drought stress. To address this, apply Muriate of Potash (0-0-60) at a rate of 100-150 grams per plant, split INTO two applications during the early and mid-stages of fruit development (e.g., May-June and August-September) to support robust cherry growth and quality.\"}', `ai_organic_alternatives` = '[\"Organic alternative 1 to address the high pH and provide nutrients IS well-rotted coffee pulp compost. Collect coffee pulp, compost it for 3-6 months in a shaded area, turning regularly to ensure aerobic decomposition. Apply 2-3 kg of mature, acidic coffee pulp compost per plant annually, mixed INTO the topsoil around the drip line, ideally during the early wet season (May-June) to slowly lower pH and provide balanced nutrients.\",\"Organic alternative 2 for Phosphorus and Potassium IS rice hull ash. Burn rice hulls in a controlled environment until they turn INTO fine ash. This ash IS rich in potassium and silica, with some phosphorus. Apply 500 grams to 1 kg of rice hull ash per plant, mixed INTO the soil around the drip line, especially during the dry season (Nov-Apr) or before flowering, to provide slow-release P and K.\",\"Organic alternative 3 for maintaining the Very High organic matter and providing balanced nutrients IS vermicast. Source high-quality vermicast FROM local producers or create your own using earthworms and organic waste. Apply 1-2 kg of vermicast per coffee plant annually, spread evenly around the base of the plant and lightly incorporated INTO the topsoil, ideally at the start of the wet season (May-June) to enhance microbial activity and nutrient availability.\"]', `ai_practices` = '[\"Practice 1: Implement deep ripping or subsoiling every 2-3 years, especially during the dry season (Nov-Apr), to break up compacted clay layers. This improves drainage, aeration, and allows coffee roots to penetrate deeper, enhancing nutrient and water uptake, which IS crucial for clay soils.\",\"Practice 2: Apply a thick layer (5-10 cm) of organic mulch using coffee pulp, rice hulls, or dried leaves around the base of each coffee tree, extending to the drip line. This should be done at the start of the dry season (November) to conserve soil moisture, suppress weeds, regulate soil temperature, and slowly release nutrients as it decomposes, supplementing the soil\'s organic matter.\",\"Practice 3: During the Philippine dry season (Nov\\u2013Apr), implement targeted drip irrigation for clay soil, which retains water well but can become hard when dry. Monitor coffee plants for signs of water stress such as wilting leaves, leaf curling, or premature cherry drop. Irrigate deeply but infrequently to encourage deep root growth, ensuring the soil IS moist but not waterlogged.\",\"Practice 4: Establish leguminous cover crops like *Flemingia macrophylla* or *Desmodium* between coffee rows. These plants improve soil structure, prevent erosion, and contribute organic matter. While nitrogen IS high, the added organic matter and improved soil health will benefit the coffee. Alternatively, plant shade trees like *Gliricidia sepium* (Kakawate) or *Acacia mangium* to regulate microclimate and contribute leaf litter, enhancing soil organic matter and nutrient cycling.\",\"Practice 5: Regularly monitor soil health between laboratory tests by observing visual signs in the coffee plants. Look for changes in leaf color (e.g., purpling for P deficiency, scorched margins for K deficiency, general yellowing for nutrient stress), growth rate, flowering intensity, and fruit SET. A new soil test should be triggered if visual symptoms persist despite following recommendations, or every 2-3 years to track long-term changes and adjust management practices.\"]', `ai_monitoring_plan` = '[\"Monitoring item 1: Every two weeks during the first application season, visually inspect coffee leaves for signs of nutrient deficiencies: look for purpling on older leaves (Phosphorus), yellowing or scorching on leaf margins (Potassium), or persistent yellowing of new leaves (Iron\\/Manganese deficiency due to high pH). If these signs appear or worsen, re-evaluate the application rates of Elemental Sulfur, Solophos, and Muriate of Potash, and consider a foliar spray for immediate relief.\",\"Monitoring item 2: After each fertilizer application, record key plant performance indicators such as the number of flower clusters per branch, the percentage of fruit SET, the average cherry size, and ultimately, the yield per tree. Use these records to assess the effectiveness of the applied nutrients and adjust the rates or timing of subsequent applications to optimize coffee production.\",\"Monitoring item 3: Conduct a follow-up soil test 6-12 months after the initial application of Elemental Sulfur and nutrient amendments, ideally after the dry season (around May). Before treatment, pH was 8.1, P was 5 ppm, and K was 10 ppm. After treatment, expect pH to gradually decrease to the 6.5-7.0 range, Phosphorus to rise to Medium (20-30 ppm), and Potassium to rise to Medium (100-150 ppm).\"]', `ai_expected_outcomes` = '', `ai_reminders` = '[]', `soil_analysis`.`updated_at` = '2026-04-04 14:46:40' WHERE `id` = 45
SELECT count(*) as aggregate FROM `soil_analysis` WHERE exists (SELECT * FROM `farmers` WHERE `soil_analysis`.`farmer_id` = `farmers`.`id` and `farmer_address_mun` = '05')
SELECT * FROM `soil_analysis` WHERE exists (SELECT * FROM `farmers` WHERE `soil_analysis`.`farmer_id` = `farmers`.`id` and `farmer_address_mun` = '05') ORDER BY `id` DESC LIMIT 10 offset 0
SELECT exists(SELECT * FROM `admin_record_views` WHERE `user_id` = 8 and `record_type` = 'soil_analysis' and `record_id` = 45) as `exists`
SELECT exists(SELECT * FROM `admin_record_views` WHERE `user_id` = 8 and `record_type` = 'soil_analysis' and `record_id` = 45) as `exists`
SELECT * FROM `admin_record_views` WHERE (`user_id` = 8 and `record_type` = 'soil_analysis' and `record_id` = 45) LIMIT 1

UPDATE `notifications` SET `read_at` = '2026-04-04 14:46:40', `notifications`.`updated_at` = '2026-04-04 14:46:40' WHERE `notifications`.`notifiable_type` = 'App\\Models\\User' and `notifications`.`notifiable_id` = 8 and `notifications`.`notifiable_id` IS not NULL and `read_at` IS NULL and `data` like '%viewRecord=45%' ORDER BY `created_at` DESC

changes.ai_key_concerns: ["Concern 1: The most critical issue is the very high soil pH of 8.1, which is far too alkaline for Arabica coffee (optimal 5.5-6.5). This high pH makes vital nutrients like Phosphorus, Potassium, Iron, Manganese, and Zinc unavailable to the coffee plants, leading to stunted growth, yellowing leaves (chlorosis), and overall poor plant vigor despite the high organic matter and nitrogen levels.","Concern 2: The extremely low Potassium (K) level of 10 ppm will severely impact coffee cherry development, bean filling, and overall yield quality. Plants will exhibit weak stems, poor disease resistance, and leaf margins may show yellowing or scorching, leading to small, light, and poor-quality coffee beans.","Concern 3: The low Phosphorus (P) level of 5 ppm is detrimental to coffee root development, flowering, and fruit set. Coffee plants will have a weak root system, reduced flower production, and poor fruit retention, resulting in significantly lower yields and delayed maturity of coffee cherries."]
changes.ai_priority_actions: ["Priority 1: Immediately apply agricultural grade Elemental Sulfur to lower the soil pH. Apply 50-100 grams per coffee plant, incorporated around the drip line, or 500-1000 kg per hectare. This product can be purchased from local agri-vet stores; estimated cost is PHP 50-100 per kg, so around PHP 2,500-10,000 for 50-100 kg for initial treatment depending on farm size.","Priority 2: Within the next two weeks, apply Muriate of Potash (0-0-60) to address the very low potassium. Apply 100-150 grams per coffee plant, split into two applications during the fruit development stage (e.g., May-June and August-September). This is available at agri-vet stores for approximately PHP 50-70 per kg.","Priority 3: Within the next 2\u20134 weeks, apply Solophos (0-20-0) or Superphosphate (0-18-0) to correct the low phosphorus. Apply 100-150 grams per coffee plant, banded around the drip line, ideally before the main flowering flush (e.g., March-April). These are available at agri-vet stores for approximately PHP 40-60 per kg."]
changes.ai_soil_remarks: {"ph":"The High pH level of 8.1 indicates an alkaline soil, which is highly unfavorable for Coffee - Arabica as it drastically reduces the availability of crucial nutrients like Phosphorus, Potassium, Iron, and Manganese, impairing root health and overall plant vigor. To correct this, the farmer must apply agricultural grade Elemental Sulfur at a rate of 50-100 grams per plant, incorporated into the soil around the drip line, to gradually lower the pH towards the optimal range of 5.5-6.5.","om":"The Very High organic matter content of 10% is excellent, significantly improving soil structure, water retention capacity, and fostering robust microbial activity, all beneficial for Coffee - Arabica. To maintain this superb level, the farmer should continue applying mature compost or vermicast at a rate of 1-2 kilograms per plant annually, ensuring a continuous supply of slow-release nutrients and sustained soil health.","n":"The Very High nitrogen level of 8.1% is excessive and can lead to undesirable effects such as overly lush vegetative growth at the expense of flowering and fruiting, making coffee plants more susceptible to pests and diseases. No additional nitrogen fertilizer is recommended at this time; instead, focus on balancing the other deficient nutrients to promote reproductive growth and avoid further N accumulation.","p":"The Low phosphorus level of 5 ppm will severely hinder coffee root development, reduce the intensity of flowering, and negatively impact fruit set, leading to lower yields and poor plant establishment. To correct this, apply Solophos (0-20-0) or Superphosphate (0-18-0) at a rate of 100-150 grams per plant, applied by banding around the drip line before the main flowering season (March-April) to ensure nutrient availability for reproductive growth.","k":"The Very Low potassium level of 10 ppm will critically affect coffee cherry filling, bean density, overall quality, and the plant's resistance to diseases and drought stress. To address this, apply Muriate of Potash (0-0-60) at a rate of 100-150 grams per plant, split into two applications during the early and mid-stages of fruit development (e.g., May-June and August-September) to support robust cherry growth and quality."}
changes.ai_organic_alternatives: ["Organic alternative 1 to address the high pH and provide nutrients is well-rotted coffee pulp compost. Collect coffee pulp, compost it for 3-6 months in a shaded area, turning regularly to ensure aerobic decomposition. Apply 2-3 kg of mature, acidic coffee pulp compost per plant annually, mixed into the topsoil around the drip line, ideally during the early wet season (May-June) to slowly lower pH and provide balanced nutrients.","Organic alternative 2 for Phosphorus and Potassium is rice hull ash. Burn rice hulls in a controlled environment until they turn into fine ash. This ash is rich in potassium and silica, with some phosphorus. Apply 500 grams to 1 kg of rice hull ash per plant, mixed into the soil around the drip line, especially during the dry season (Nov-Apr) or before flowering, to provide slow-release P and K.","Organic alternative 3 for maintaining the Very High organic matter and providing balanced nutrients is vermicast. Source high-quality vermicast from local producers or create your own using earthworms and organic waste. Apply 1-2 kg of vermicast per coffee plant annually, spread evenly around the base of the plant and lightly incorporated into the topsoil, ideally at the start of the wet season (May-June) to enhance microbial activity and nutrient availability."]
changes.ai_practices: ["Practice 1: Implement deep ripping or subsoiling every 2-3 years, especially during the dry season (Nov-Apr), to break up compacted clay layers. This improves drainage, aeration, and allows coffee roots to penetrate deeper, enhancing nutrient and water uptake, which is crucial for clay soils.","Practice 2: Apply a thick layer (5-10 cm) of organic mulch using coffee pulp, rice hulls, or dried leaves around the base of each coffee tree, extending to the drip line. This should be done at the start of the dry season (November) to conserve soil moisture, suppress weeds, regulate soil temperature, and slowly release nutrients as it decomposes, supplementing the soil's organic matter.","Practice 3: During the Philippine dry season (Nov\u2013Apr), implement targeted drip irrigation for clay soil, which retains water well but can become hard when dry. Monitor coffee plants for signs of water stress such as wilting leaves, leaf curling, or premature cherry drop. Irrigate deeply but infrequently to encourage deep root growth, ensuring the soil is moist but not waterlogged.","Practice 4: Establish leguminous cover crops like *Flemingia macrophylla* or *Desmodium* between coffee rows. These plants improve soil structure, prevent erosion, and contribute organic matter. While nitrogen is high, the added organic matter and improved soil health will benefit the coffee. Alternatively, plant shade trees like *Gliricidia sepium* (Kakawate) or *Acacia mangium* to regulate microclimate and contribute leaf litter, enhancing soil organic matter and nutrient cycling.","Practice 5: Regularly monitor soil health between laboratory tests by observing visual signs in the coffee plants. Look for changes in leaf color (e.g., purpling for P deficiency, scorched margins for K deficiency, general yellowing for nutrient stress), growth rate, flowering intensity, and fruit set. A new soil test should be triggered if visual symptoms persist despite following recommendations, or every 2-3 years to track long-term changes and adjust management practices."]
changes.ai_monitoring_plan: ["Monitoring item 1: Every two weeks during the first application season, visually inspect coffee leaves for signs of nutrient deficiencies: look for purpling on older leaves (Phosphorus), yellowing or scorching on leaf margins (Potassium), or persistent yellowing of new leaves (Iron\/Manganese deficiency due to high pH). If these signs appear or worsen, re-evaluate the application rates of Elemental Sulfur, Solophos, and Muriate of Potash, and consider a foliar spray for immediate relief.","Monitoring item 2: After each fertilizer application, record key plant performance indicators such as the number of flower clusters per branch, the percentage of fruit set, the average cherry size, and ultimately, the yield per tree. Use these records to assess the effectiveness of the applied nutrients and adjust the rates or timing of subsequent applications to optimize coffee production.","Monitoring item 3: Conduct a follow-up soil test 6-12 months after the initial application of Elemental Sulfur and nutrient amendments, ideally after the dry season (around May). Before treatment, pH was 8.1, P was 5 ppm, and K was 10 ppm. After treatment, expect pH to gradually decrease to the 6.5-7.0 range, Phosphorus to rise to Medium (20-30 ppm), and Potassium to rise to Medium (100-150 ppm)."]

GeminiService: JSON parse failed. Raw: {
  "diagnosis": "The Ababon Fely Farm's Clay soil exhibits a complex nutrient profile, characterized by an alarmingly high pH of 8.1, coupled with critically low levels of Phosphorus (5 ppm) and Potassium (10 ppm). The most critical imbalance is the high pH, which severely limits the availability a

data.*tooltip: Uses Google Gemini AI to generate a recommendation. Result will appear in the AI Recommendation PDF viewer below.
Uses Google Gemini AI to generate a recommendation. Result will appear in the AI Recommendation PDF viewer below.
